title: श्यामसुन्दर स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री श्यामसुन्दर स्तोत्रम् ॥

श्यामसुन्दरं देवं वंशीधरमनुपमम्।
पीताम्बरधरं कृष्णं वन्दे वृन्दावनेश्वरम्॥ १॥

मुरलीमधुरध्वानं कुञ्जबिहारिणं हरिम्।
रासमण्डलमध्यस्थं गोपिकाप्रेमवल्लभम्॥ २॥

मेघश्यामलवर्णाभं पूर्णचन्द्रनिभाननम्।
कुण्डलद्वयशोभाढ्यं कौस्तुभाभरणान्वितम्॥ ३॥

नवनीतविलिप्ताङ्गं गोपालं गोचरायणम्।
यशोदाहृदयानन्दं नन्दगोपकुलोद्भवम्॥ ४॥

गोवर्धनधरं वीरं कालियफणिनर्तकम्।
माखनचौरमीशानं बालगोपालमद्भुतम्॥ ५॥

राधामाधवमव्यक्तं प्रेमभक्तिप्रदायकम्।
वृन्दावनविहारिणं यमुनातटसुन्दरम्॥ ६॥

पद्मनाभं हृषीकेशमच्युतं शार्ङ्गधन्विनम्।
वासुदेवं जगन्नाथं मुकुन्दं मुरमर्दनम्॥ ७॥

श्यामसुन्दरसस्तोत्रं यः पठेद् भक्तितत्परः।
कृष्णप्रेमरसासक्तो वैकुण्ठगतिमाप्नुयात्॥ ८॥

॥ इति श्री श्यामसुन्दर स्तोत्रम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Shyama Sundara Stotram ||

Shyamasundaram Devam Vamshidharam Anupamam |
Pitambaradharam Krishnam Vande Vrindavaneshvaram || 1 ||

Muralimadhuradhvanam Kunjabiaharinam Harim |
Rasamandalamadhyastham Gopikaprema Vallabham || 2 ||

Meghashyamalavarnabham Purnachandranibhananam |
Kundaladvayashobhadhyam Kaustubhabharananvitam || 3 ||

Navaneetaviliptangam Gopalam Gocharayanam |
Yashodahridayanamdam Nandagopaukulodbhavam || 4 ||

Govardhaanadharam Viram Kaliyaphaninartakam |
Makhanachauramishaanam Balagopalamaddhutam || 5 ||

Radhamadhavam Avyaktam Premabhaktipradayakam |
Vrindavanaviaharinam Yamunatatasundaram || 6 ||

Padmanabham Hrishikesham Achyutam Sharngadhanvinam |
Vasudevam Jagannatham Mukundam Muramardanam || 7 ||

Shyamasundarastotram Yah Pathed Bhaktitatparah |
Krishnapremarasaasakto Vaikunthagatim Aapnuyat || 8 ||

|| Iti Shri Shyama Sundara Stotram Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
श्यामसुन्दर, वंशीधर, पीताम्बरधर, वृन्दावनेश्वर कृष्ण को वन्दन।

मुरली-मधुरध्वनि, कुञ्जविहारी, रासमण्डलमध्यस्थ, गोपिकाप्रेमवल्लभ।

मेघश्यामवर्ण, पूर्णचन्द्रमुख, कुण्डलशोभित, कौस्तुभभूषित।

नवनीतविलिप्तांग, गोपाल, माखनचोर, बालगोपाल।

राधामाधव, प्रेमभक्तिप्रदायक, यमुनातट सुन्दर।

भक्ति से पढ़ने वाला कृष्णप्रेमरस में आसक्त हो वैकुण्ठ गति पाता है।
benefits: Beautiful hymn to Shyama Sundara Krishna of Vrindavan
Invokes the mesmerizing flute-playing form of Krishna
Captures Bal-Gopal, Raas-Krishna, and Dwarkadhish forms
Ideal for Janmashtami, Holi, and daily Krishna worship
description: Shyama Sundara Stotram — श्यामसुन्दर स्तोत्रम् — is a devotional hymn of praise offered to Lord Krishna. This sacred composition is sourced from Garga Samhita and continues to inspire devotees worldwide. Regular recitation is believed to invoke the mesmerizing flute-playing form of Krishna, capture Bal-Gopal, Raas-Krishna, and Dwarkadhish forms, and beautiful hymn to Shyama Sundara Krishna of Vrindavan. It is especially recommended on Wednesday (Budhvar) and during Janmashtami and Holi for maximum spiritual benefit. At 8 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 4 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Shyama Sundara Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Shyama Sundara Stotram in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Beautiful hymn to Krishna's enchanting Vrindavan form for divine love.
relatedSearches: Shyama Sundara Krishna meaning dark beautiful

seoFaqs.question: What does the name 'Shyama Sundara' mean and to whom does it refer?
seoFaqs.answer: Shyama Sundara is a compound Sanskrit epithet of Krishna, formed from shyama (dark, dark-blue, dark-cloud-coloured) and sundara (beautiful). Together the name means 'the beautiful dark-hued one' and refers specifically to Krishna in his Vraja (Vrindavan) form, the youthful cowherd whose complexion is described in the Bhagavata Purana and Brahma Samhita as resembling a fresh rain cloud or a tamala tree. The name is one of the most affectionate Vaishnava titles of Krishna and is invoked across Gaudiya, Pushti Marg and Madhva traditions. Hymns titled or themed around Shyama Sundara therefore belong to the Krishna-bhakti corpus and emphasise Krishna's accessible, intimate, beautiful aspect rather than his cosmic or sovereign forms.

seoFaqs.question: Is there a single canonical Sanskrit text titled 'Shyama Sundara Stotram'?
seoFaqs.answer: There is no single universally cited Puranic chapter under this exact title. The name circulates as a popular devotional designation for hymns whose central refrain or invocation is 'Shyama Sundara'. The Sai Rhythms tradition preserves a 'Ghana Shyama Sundara' bhajan that addresses Krishna as the cloud-dark beautiful Lord, son of Devaki and foster-son of Nanda, refuge of the downtrodden, and Lord of Dwaraka. Sanskrit-document repositories such as sanskritdocuments.org list multiple Shyama-themed Krishna stotras under varied titles. Devotees encountering this slug should treat it as a Krishna-bhakti hymn of the Vraja-emphasis genre and consult their lineage acharya for the recension followed locally, since the Gaudiya, Pushti Marg and Madhva traditions each maintain slightly different versions.

seoFaqs.question: Why is Krishna described as Shyama (dark) rather than fair?
seoFaqs.answer: The Bhagavata Purana, Vishnu Purana and Brahma Samhita all describe Krishna's complexion as shyama, comparable to a freshly rain-laden monsoon cloud, the petal of a blue lotus, or the tamala tree's dark trunk. Theologically this dark hue carries multiple meanings: it signals Krishna's identification with the cosmic depth and mystery (just as the night sky and deep ocean appear dark), it provides the chromatic contrast that allows his yellow pitambara (silk garment), peacock feather and gold ornaments to shine, and it represents his role as the absorber of devotees' impurities. Vaishnava aesthetic theory holds that shyama is the supreme rasa-evoking colour for the Krishna-lila precisely because it allows infinite contemplation. The Shyama Sundara epithet thus celebrates this theologically loaded chromatic choice.

seoFaqs.question: Which classical Krishna stotras are most closely related to the Shyama Sundara theme?
seoFaqs.answer: The Krishnashtakam (eight-verse Krishna hymn) attributed to Adi Shankaracharya is the most closely related canonical text, with multiple verses emphasising Krishna's dark beauty and Vrindavan childhood. The Madhurashtakam by Vallabhacharya celebrates Krishna's all-pervading madhurya (sweetness) and is sung at every Shrinathji haveli. The Nandakumar Ashtakam praises Krishna as the son of Nanda with detailed iconographic description. The Govinda Damodara Stotra by Bilvamangala is a longer hymn invoking Krishna's many names. The Krishna sections of the Bhagavata Mahatmya and the daily Vaishnava nitya-paath also include Shyama-themed verses. Devotees seeking a complete Shyama Sundara contemplation often combine excerpts from these allied hymns rather than relying on a single labelled text.

seoFaqs.question: How and when are Shyama Sundara Krishna hymns traditionally recited?
seoFaqs.answer: These hymns are recited daily as part of nitya-puja in Vaishnava households, on Janmashtami (Krishna's birth), Nandotsav (the day after), Radhashtami, Govardhan Puja, Holi and the four months of Chaturmas. In Gaudiya tradition they accompany the morning mangala-arati and evening sandhya-arati. In Pushti Marg they form part of the eight-darshan seva at every haveli. Domestic devotees typically chant a single round during morning puja before a Krishna or Radha-Krishna image, with extended parayana on the festival days listed. The hymns are usually sung in melodic kirtan style rather than recited monotonically, since the Shyama-Sundara genre belongs to the rasa-lila aesthetic in which musical sweetness is itself part of the seva.

seoFaqs.question: What is the typical structure and length of a Shyama Sundara hymn?
seoFaqs.answer: Most Shyama Sundara hymns are short, ranging from eight verses (ashtakam) to sixteen names (shodashanama) to longer thirty-verse compositions. The eight-verse format is the most common. Each verse typically opens with an invocation such as 'shyama shyama' or 'ghana shyama sundara' and proceeds through Krishna's iconographic features (peacock feather, yellow garment, flute, lotus eyes), his Vraja relationships (with Yashoda, Nanda, the gopis, Radha), and his cosmic identity (Govinda, Govardhana-dhari, Madhusudana). Recitation of an eight-verse hymn at moderate tempo takes approximately 2 to 4 minutes, allowing daily inclusion in any puja sequence. Closing phala-shruti verses promise that regular recitation cultivates Krishna-bhakti, removes obstacles to devotional progress, and grants ultimate proximity to Krishna's divine play.
